verses.text: The words of the Ecclesiasta, son of Dauid,

king of Israel in Ierousalem. Vanity of vanities, said the Ecclesiast, Vanity of vanities! All things are vanity. What surplus exists for people in all their toil at which they toil under the sun? A generation goes, and a generation comes, and the earth stands forever.
The sun rises, and the sun goes down and draws along to its place.

When it rises there, it goes to the south and circles to the north. It circles, going in circles—sob proceeds the spiritc, and on its circuits the spiritc returns. All the wadis go to the sea, and the sea will not be full; to the place where the wadis go, there they turn back to go.
All words are dworn outd;

a man will not be able to speak; the eye will not be satisfied with seeing, and the ear will not be filled with hearing. What is that which has happened? It is that which will happen! And what is that which has been done? It is that which will be done. And there is nothing novel under the sun.

it has already happened in the ages As for the person who will speak and say, “See, this is new!”— aI.e. a member of an Assembly bLacking in Gk cOr win of ecclesiast le, according to the approach of the Greek translaoj and all 10 instances of #y) are rendered by a)nh/r. o track the differences between Md) and #y) in the gs may be maintained for a1nqrwpoj, a)nh/r must be ions as an indefinite pronoun, in order to represent also instances where gender inclusive language has ular pronouns and verbs to common gender plural ns to passive. Sometimes this practice so alters the that the formal equivalence between the Greek and clesiast 4.14 and 8.12 may illustrate the attempt in reproduces the Hebrew text. beginning and ending of sentences or connections RSV, in essence the same versification is used. Difpunctuation. reek adapted to NRSV. Occasionally, the translation PETER J. GENTRY that have been before us.
I, the Ecclesiast, was king over Israel in Ierousalem.
And I applied my heart to seek out and to examine by wisdom concerning all things that happen under heaven, for an unhappy preoccupatione God has given to human beings with which to be preoccupied.

I saw all the deeds that have been done under the sun, and see, all are vanity and preference of spirit. There is no remembrance of earlier people, indeed, of those born later— there will not be a remembrance of them with those who will be born at the last.
Something crooked will not be able to be set in order, and what is lacking will not be able to be counted.
I spoke in my heart by saying, “As for me, see, I have become great and have added wisdom to all who were before me in Ierousalem, and my heart saw many things regarding wisdom and knowledge.”
And I applied my heart to know wisdom and knowledge; derangements and understanding I understood, that, indeed, this is preference of spirit.
and those who increase knowledge will increase suffering.

I said in my heart, “Come now; let me test you with enjoyment; see in good!” And see, indPossibly overworked eOr distraction For in a great quantity of wisdom is a great quantity of knowledge, ecc deed, this was vanity.
Of laughter I said, “ ness,” and of enjoyment, “Why are you doing t

I looked about in my heart whether to draw flesh into wine—and my heart guided in wisdo and whether to lay hold on folly until I might what kind of thing is good for human beings t under the sun, the number of the days of their
I made my work great; I built houses for mys planted vineyards for myself.
I made myself dens and parks, and I planted in them every tree.
I made myself pools of water to water f them a grove sprouting trees.
I acquired male female slaves, and I had homebred; indeed, I great possessions of herds and flocks, more tha who had been before me in Ierousalem.
I gath for myself, indeed, silver and gold and valued sessions of kings and of the territories; I got singers and female singers and the delight human beings, a cupbearer and pitchersa.
And I became great and increased more t all who were before me in Ierousalem; indeed, wisdom stood firm for me.
Anything for w my eyes begged, I did not take away from the did not hinder my heart from any enjoyment, cause my heart rejoiced in all my toil and this my portion from all my toil.
And I looked a my works that my hands had done and at the wherein I toiled to do itb and see, all were va and preference of spirit, and there is no sur under the sun.
And I looked to see wisdom and mad and folly, for who is the person who will com follow the planc in as many things as he mad
And I saw that wisdom excels folly as light e darkness.
As for the wise, their eyes are in their he and the fool goes in darkness, And I know, indeed I, that one eventuality befall them all.
And I said in my heart, “As it falls the fool, indeed, it will befall me, and have I become wise?” I then spoke excessivel my heart, because a fool will speak from ex that, indeed, this is vanity.
For there is n membrance ever of the wise with the fool, i much as already the coming days, even all thi are forgotten, and how will the wise die with fool?
And I hated life, because the work th done under the sun was grievous to me, fo things are vanity and preference of spirit.

I hated all my toil in which I am toi under the sun, because I am leaving it to the pe being born after me 19—and who knows whe he will be wise or foolish? And he will have thority over all my toil at which I toiled and came wise under the sun; indeed, this is va
And I turned to bid farewell with my heart t the toil at which I toiled under the sun.
For t is a person whose toil is with wisdom and kn edge and manliness, and a person who did not at it: he will give him his portion; indeed, th vanity and a great evil.

For what happens aPossibly female cupbearers bLacking in Gk cOr co humans than fPerhaps nothing better for them esiast 2–3 651 adis?” my — see do life. lf; I garruit om nd ad all red osale of an my ich; I bewas all toil ity lus mortals in all their toil and in the preference of their heart with which they toil under the sun?

For all their days are daysb of pains, and their preoccupation is of anger; indeed, at night their heart does not sleep. Indeed, this is vanity. 24 eIt is not good in humanse what they will eat and what they will drink and what they will show to their soul as good in their toil. Indeed, this, I saw, is from the hand of God.
For who will eat and who will refrain from eatingb apart from him?

For to the one who is good before him he gave wisdom and knowledge and enjoyment, and to the sinner he gave a preoccupation to add and to gather, to give to the one who is good before God, because, indeed, this is vanity and preference of spirit.
For everything there is a time, and a right time for every matter under heaven: ess to it. cels

a right time to give birth, and a right time to die; a right time to plant, and a right time to pluck up what is planted; a right time to kill, and a right time to heal; a right time to tear down, and a right time to build; a right time to weep, and a right time to laugh; a right time to mourn, and a right time to dance; a right time to throw stones, and a right time to gather stones; a right time to embrace, and a right time to be far from embracing; a right time to seek, and a right time to lose; a right time to keep, and a right time to throw away; a right time to tear, and a right time to sew; a right time to keep silence, and a right time to speak; a right time to love, and a right time to hate; a right time for war, and a right time for peace.
What surplus has the maker in what he toils? d, ill behy in ess, reasgs, the t is all ing son her aubeity. all ere wltoil s is to nsel
I saw all the preoccupation which God has given to humans to be preoccupied with.
He made everything fine in its right time; indeed, he granted eternity in their heart so that they should not find the work that God has done from the beginning even to the end.
I know that there is fno good in themf except to be glad and to do good in their life.
Indeed, everyone who will eat and drink and should see good in their toil—it is a divine gift.
I know that everything that God has done—it will be forever; to it there is nothing to add, and from it there is nothing to take away; God has done this so that all should stand in awe before him.
That which happened, already is, and what is to happen, already took place, and God will seek out the one being pursued.
Moreover, I saw under the sun the place of justice—the impious was there, and the place of dPerhaps belongs ePerhaps There is nothing better in 652 eccles the righteous—the impious was there.
I said in my heart, God will judge the righteous and the impious, for there is a right time for every matter and for every work.
There I said in my heart concerning the speech of human beings, that God will discern them, indeed, to show even to them that they are cattle.

For what befalls humans and what befalls cattle—one eventuality is for them; as the death of the one, so is the death of the other. They all have one spirita, and in what way did humans excel above cattle? Nothing, because all things are vanity.
All things go to one place; all things came from the dust, and all things return to the dust.
And who knows the spirit of humans, whether it ascends upward, and the spirit of cattle, whether it descends downward to the earth?
And I saw that there is bno goodb except that people will enjoy themselves in their works, because that is their portion, for who will bring them to see whatever happens after them?

And I turned, and I saw all the extortions that take place under the sun. Look, the tear of the oppressed—and there is no one to comfort them! From the hand of those oppressing them is strength—and there is no one to comfort them.
And I praised the dead, who have already died, more than the living who are living until now.
And better than both is whoever has not yet been, who has not seen the grievous work that is done under the sun.
And I saw all toil and all manliness of work, that it is a man’s envy of his companion. Indeed, this is vanity and preference of spirit.
Fools folded their hands and consumed their own flesh. Better is a handful of repose than two handfuls of toil, and preference of spirit.

And I turned and saw vanity under the sun: 8there is one, and there is no second; indeed, he has neither son nor brother, and there is no end to all his toil; indeed, his eye is not satisfied with riches. “For whom do I toil and deprive my life from goodness?” Even this is vanity and an unhappy preoccupation.
Better are two than one, because they have a good reward in their toil.
For if they fall, the one will raise his partner up, and woe to the one, when he falls, and there should be no second to raise him up.
Indeed, if two sleep, they also have warmth, and the one, how should he be warmed?
Even if the one should prevail, two will stand against him. A threefold cord will not be quickly broken.

Better a child poor and wise than a king old and foolish, who did not know to give heed any longer; 14for from the house of prisoners hec will come out to reign, for, indeed, in his kingdom he was born poor.
I saw all the living, who were walking about under the sun, with the second youth, who will stand in hisd place.

There is no aPossibly breath bPerhaps nothing better cI.e. the child ast 3–5 end to all the people, to all who were born before them; indeed, those who came later will not rejoice in him, for, indeed, this is vanity and prefer-
Guard your foot, whenever you go to the house of God and are near to hear. Above a gift of fools is your sacrifice, for they do not know how to do evil.

(2)Never be in a hurry with your mouth, nor let your heart be quick to utter a word before God, for God is in heaven and you upon earth; therefore let your words be few. (3) Foradreamcomeswithagreatamountof distractione, and a fool’s voice with a great quantity of words. (4) WheneveryoumakeavowtoGod,donot delay to fulfill it, for there is no will in fools. Whatever you vow, pay up. (5)It is better that you do not vow than that you vow and do not pay up. (6)Do not allow your mouth to lead your flesh into sin, and do not say before God, “It is ignorance,” in order that God should not be angry at your utterance and destroy the works of your hands.
(7) For with a great quantity of dreams are both vanities and many words; so fear God!
(8) Ifyouseeinaterritorytheextortionofthe poor and the snatching away of justice and right, do not be amazed at the matter, for there is a high one to watch over a high one and higher ones over them.
(9)And the surplus of the land in everything is a king over the worked field.
(10) One who loves money will not be satisfied with money. And who loved produce in a great quantity of them? Indeed, this is vanity.
(11) With an abundance of goodness, eaters of it were increased. And what is manliness to him who has it, except to see with his eyes?
(12) Sweet is the sleep of a slave, whether he eats little or much, and for the person filled full with being rich, there is no sending him away to sleep.
(13) There is an illness that I saw under the sun: riches being kept by him who has them to his misfortune.
(14)And those riches will be lost in an unhappy preoccupation, and he begot a child, and there is nothing in his hand.
(15)Just as he came out from his mother’s womb naked, he will return to go as he came, and he will not take anything for his toil that may go in his hand.
(16)Indeed, this is a grievous ill: for as he arrived, so also he will go away, and what is his surplus, at which he toils for wind?
(17)Indeed, all his days are in darkness and mourning, and with much anger and sickness and bitternessf.

(18) See what I saw as good, what is fine: to eat and to drink and to see goodness in all his toil at whatever he toils under the sun, the number of the days of his life which God gave to him, for this is his portion.

(19)Indeed, all to whom God has given to them wealth and possessions and they have authority over them to eat from it and to take I.e. the king's eOr preoccupation fOr resentment ecc their portion and to enjoy themselves in t toil—this is a divine gift.

(20)For not in man spects will they remember the days of their l because God keeps them preoccupied with merriment of their hearts. There is an evil that I have seen under the and it is great upon humankind: there man to whom God will give to him wealth possessions and honor, and he is not lacking respect to his soul from all things that he des and God does not give authority to him to from it, because a stranger will eat it. This is va and a grievous illness. If a man begets a hun children and will live many years and a great n ber is what will be the days of his years and soul will not be satisfied with goodness, indee has no burial; I said: A miscarriagea is better t he. For it came in vanity, and it goes in dark and in darkness its name will be covered; 5ind it did not see the sun and did not know; this has more rest than he. Even if he lived recurre of a thousand years and he did not see goodne do not all things go to one place?
All toil of humankind is for their mouth deed, their soul will not be satisfied.
For surplus has the wise over the fool, since the n knows to walk before life?
Better is the sight o eyes than going on spiritb; indeed, this is va and preference of spirit.
If anything has happened, it has alr been named, and it is known what human be are, and they will not be able to dispute with t stronger than they.
For many words are multi ing vanity; what surplus has one?

For who kn what is good for mortals in their life, the nu of the days of their life of vanity, and they pa them in shadow? For who will tell them what be after them under the sun? A good name is better than good anointing-oil, and the day of death is better than the day of one’s birth. It is better to go to a house of mourning than that to go to a house of drinking because this is the end of everyone, and the living will give it to his heart. Better is anger than laughter, for by a bad countenance the heart wi be made good. The heart of the wise is in the house of mourning, and the heart of fools is in the house mirth. It is better to hear the rebuke of the wise than a man hearing the song of fools. For as the sound of thorns under a cauldron, so is the laughter of fools; indeed, this is vanity. For extortion turns a wise person mad, aOr premature stillbirth bI.e. courage cOr settle dP esiast 5–7 653 eir re- 8 ves, the and it will destroy his heart of courage. Better is the end of words than its beginning; better is a patient person than one proud in spirit. Do not be hasty in your spirit to become un, angry, c is a nd ith res, eat ity red mhis, he an ess, ed, ne ces s— inhat edy the ity ady ngs ose lyws ber sed ill

for anger will rest in the bosom of fools. Do not say, “Why did it happen that the former days were better than these?” For it is not with wisdom you have asked concerning this. Wisdom is good with distribution of landd, and a surplus to those who see the sun. For wisdom, in its shade, is like the shade of money, and a surplus of knowledge of wisdom will give life to the one who has it. Consider the works of God; who will be able to set in order whomever God has made crooked? l f

In a day of prosperity live in prosperity, and in a day of misfortune, consider; indeed, the one in proportion to the other God has made concerning speech so that mortals may not find anything after them.
I saw all things in my days of vanity; there are righteous people perishing in their righteousness, and there are impious people remaining in their wickedness.
Do not be very righteous, and do not be excessively wise, lest you be horrified.
Do not be very ungodly, and do not become hard so that you should not die when it is not your time.
It is good that you hold on to the one; indeed, do not let your hand go from the other, for the one who fears God will go forth in all things.
Wisdom will give help to the wise more than ten having authority over those who are in the city.
Forastohumanity,thereisnotajustperson in the earth who will do good and will not sin.

Indeed, to all the words that people will speak do not give your heart so that you do not hear your slave cursing you, 22for frequently he will do you evil and many times he will bring hurt to your heart as, indeed, you have cursed others.
AllthesethingsItestedbywisdom;Isaid,“I will become wise,” 24and it was far from me, far beyond what was, and a deep depth, who will find it?
I—in fact my heart—went full circle to know and to examine and seek wisdom and a reckoning and to know the folly of the impious and hardness and madness.

And I find more bitter than death the woman who is hunted prey, and her heart is hunting-nets; her hands are fetters; one who is good before God will be delivered from her, and one sinning will be taken by her.

See, this I found, said the Ecclesiast, adding one to one to find the calculation 28which my soul still sought but did not find. One man from a thousand I found, and a woman among all these I did not find.
Albeit, see, this I found what God made: ssibly inheritance 654 eccles man upright. And they themselves have searched out many schemesa.
Who knows the wise? And who knows the interpretation of what is said? A person’s wisdom will illuminate his face, and one without shame in his face will be hated.
Keep a king’s utterance, and do not be hasty about the expression of a sacred oath.
From his presence you will go; do not stand still because of painful words, for all that he wants he will do.
Just as a king will speak having authority, and who will say to him, “What will you do?”
Whoever keeps a command will not know anything harmful, and the heart of the wise knows the right time of decision.
For to every matter there is a right time and decision, for the knowledge of mortals is great upon them.
For they do not know what is going to be, for who will tell them just how it will be?

There is no one having authority over the spirit to restrain the spirit, and there is no authority over the day of death; there is no discharge from the battle, and impiety will not deliver those at home with it.
All this I observed, and I gave my heart to every work that is done under the sun— things that one person exercised authority over another to hurt them.
Then I saw the impious carried into tombs; they went from the holy place, and they were praised in the city that they did so. Indeed, this is vanity.
Because a refutation of those doing evil there does not happen quickly—on account of this the heart of humans was fully convinced in them to do evil.

He who sinned did what was evil from then and from ca length to itc; for, indeed, I know that it will be well with those who fear God so that they should fear before him, 13and it will not be well for the impious, and they will not prolong days in shadow who do not stand in fear before God.

There is a vanity that is done on earth, that there are righteous people that it overtakes them according to the work of the impious, and there are ungodly that it overtakes them according as the work of the righteous. I said that, indeed, this is vanity.

And I praised enjoyment, for there is dno goodd for people under the sun except to eat and to drink and to enjoy themselves, and this will be there with them in their toil through the days of their life that God has given to them under the sun.

When I gave my heart to know wisdom and to see the preoccupation that is done on earth, because, indeed, by day and by night there is no seeing sleep with one’s eyes, 17then I saw all the works of God, that no one will be able to find out the work that is done under the sun; as much as one may toil to search out, he will not find; indeed, aOr reasonings bOr solution cPossibly a long time before whom you made love gOr thought hPerhaps great siege wo b ast 8–9 whatever the wise say they know, they will not be able to find it out. For all this I gave to my heart, and my heart saw all this: how the righteous and the wise and their deedse are in the hand of God; indeed, love and, indeed, hate there is no person in the know; all things are before them; 2vanity is in all things. One eventuality is for the righteous and the impious, for the good and for the evil, for the clean and for the unclean, for the one who sacrifices and for the one who does not sacrifice. As are the good, so are the sinners; so are those who swear, just as those who fear an oath. This is an evil in all that is done under the sun, that one eventuality is for all. Indeed, the heart of humans was filled with evil; madness is in their heart while they live, and after them they go to the dead. For who are they who have a part with all the living? There is hope, because a living dog is better than a dead lion. For the living will know that they will die, and the dead are not knowing anything; they have no more reward, because their memory is forgotten. Indeed, their love and their hate and their jealousy have already perished, and they have no further portion forever in all that is done under the sun. Go, eat your bread with enjoyment, and drink your wine with a good heart, for God has already approved your works. At every time let your garments be white; do let not oil be lacking upon your head. See life with the wife fwhom you lovedf, all the days of your life of vanity that are given to you under the sun—all your days of vanity, because it is your portion in your life and in your toil at which you toil under the sun.
Whatever your hand finds to do, as is your might, do! For there is no work and reasoningg and knowledge and wisdom in Hades, where you are going.

I turned, and I saw under the sun that the race is not to the nimble, nor the battle to the strong, nor, indeed, bread to the wise, nor, indeed, riches to the intelligent, nor, indeed, favor to those who are perceptive, because time and chance will happen to them all.

For, indeed, no one knows their time. Like fish being taken with a pernicious net and like birds being caught in a snare, like them mortals are snared at a time of ruin, when it suddenly falls upon them.
Indeed, this I saw as wisdom under the sun, and it was great according to me.

There was a little city and few men in it, and a great king would come against it and surround it and would build ha great palisaded camph against it, 15and he would find in it a poor wise man, and he himself will deliver the city by his wisdom. And no one remembered that poor man.
And I said, “Better is wisdom than might, and the poor man’s wisdom is despised, and his words are not being heard.”
The words of the wise in rest are heard above dPerhaps nothing better eOr productions fPossibly with ks ecc

the shouting of those exercising autho in follies. Wisdom is better than instruments of wa and one sinner will destroy much goo Deadly flies will make a preparation of aromatic oil give off a foul odor; a little wisdom is of more worth than honor derived from great folly. The heart of the wise is to his right, and the heart of the fool is to his left. Indeed, when a fool walks on the road, heart will be lacking, and all he will consider is foolishness. If the spirit of the one holding authority rises against you, do not leave your place, for healing will put a stop to great offens There is an evil, which I saw under the like an involuntary offense, which proceeded f the one in authority: 6a fool was allowed in heights, and the rich will sit in a low place. I seen slaves upon horses and rulers walking on ground like slaves.

and he will put power into his efforts, and the extra of being a man is skill. If the snake bites when there is no whisperingc, in fact there is no surplus to the char The words of the mouth of the wise are grace, and the lips of the fool will plunge hi into the sea. The beginning of the words of his mout foolishness, and the last of his speech is wicked madness, and the fool multiplies words. No one knows what has happened, and what will be after them who can t them? The toil of fools will weary them— one who did not know how to go int the city. Alas for you, O city, whose king is young and your rulers eat in the early morni Happy are you, O land, whose king is th son of freemen, and your rulers at the proper time wil din strengthd, The one digging a hole will fall into it, and the one who demolishes a wall— snake will bite him. One who lifts stones will be hurta by the one who splits trees will be endangere by them. If the iron tool failsb, he himself has als troubled his face, and they will not be ashamed. aPossibly worn out bOr becomes dilapidated fOr that gOr evil cI.e. by esiast 9–12 655 ity, .

Through hesitations the roofing will be debasede, and in idleness of hands the house will drip. They make bread for laughter; wine gladdens the living and will obey money in all respects. Indeed, in your consciousness do not curse the king, and in your bedrooms do not curse the rich, for a bird of the sky will carry off your voice, and one having wings will bring back word. is s. un, om reat ave the Send out your bread upon the face of the water, er. is ll g! eat spirit is, like bones in the belly of the pregnant woman, so you will not know the works of God, whatever he will do. In the morning, sow your seed, and to the evening do not let your hand be slack, for you do not know what sort will fit, whether this or that and whether the two are good together. Light is sweet, and it is good for the eyes to see the sun. For even if a person will live many years, in them all he will be merry, and he will remember the days of darkness, becausef they will be many. All that is coming is vanity. Rejoice, young man, in your youth, and let your heart do you good in the days of your youth. Walk in the ways of your heart and by the sight of your eyes, and know that for all these things God will bring you into judgment. Put away anger from your heart, and divert paing from your body, for youth and lack of understanding are vanity.
Remember your creator in the days of your youth, before the days of trouble come and the years arrive in which you will say, “I have no wish for them.”
Before the sun and the light and the moon and the stars are darkened and the

for in a great number of days you will find it. Give a portion to seven, indeed, to eight, for you do not know what bad condition will be upon the earth. If the clouds are full of rain, upon the earth they will pour out; whether a tree falls to the south or to the north, in the place where the tree will fall, there it will be. One who watches the wind will not sow, and one who looks at the clouds will not reap.

Whenonedoesnotknowwhatthewayofthe; a snake-charmer dPossibly with gusto ePossibly deteriorated 656 eccle clouds return after the rain; 3in the day when the guards of the house shake and the strong men are twisted, and the women who grind ceased working, because they are few, and those who look through the windows will be dimmed, 4and they will lock the doors in the market, because of the weakness of the sound of the woman who grinds, and one will rise up at the sound of the sparrow, and all the daughters of song will be brought low. Indeed, they will see from heights, and terrors will be in the road; when the almond tree blossoms, the grasshopper becomes fat, and the caperberry is scattered, because mortals went to their eternal home, and the mourners circled in the market; 6before the cord of silvera is ruined and the blossom of gold is crushed and the pitcher is broken at the fountain and the wheel at the cistern races together 7and the dust returns to the earth as it was and the spirit returns to the God aOr money bPossibly shepherd. And beyond them, my son iast 12 who gave it.
Vanity of vanities, said the Ecclesiast; all is vanity.
Beyond the fact that the Ecclesiast was wise, he also taught the people knowledge, and an ear will track out an arrangement of parables.
The Ecclesiast sought many ways to find the words he wanted, even something written of straightforwardness, words of truth.
The words of the wise are like ox-goads and like nails firmly planted; those from the collections were given from one bshepherd and a surplus of them.
My sonb, beware of making many books; there is no end, and much study is a wearying of the flesh.
The end of the message; all is heard. Fear God, and keep his commandments, for this is every person.

For God will bring every work in judgment, in everything overlooked, whether good or whether evil.
